analyst_comment: November events were delivered with in-house crew and staging stock instead of the subcontracting assumed in budget; Q4 freight was also renegotiated. Genuine savings, not a timing shift. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-12-10)

An unplanned client project was won in late March and delivered inside April; external production was booked at short-notice premium rates. Together with the matching revenue line the net margin impact is slightly negative. Change-order discipline flagged to the project director. (FP&A Analyst, 2026-05-12)
A client brought part of its September roadshow forward into July. Timing between months rather than new business; the September shortfall stayed within tolerance. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-08-11)
Unbudgeted revenue from the same late-won April project; see the matching COGS variance. Recognized on delivery. (FP&A Analyst, 2026-05-12)
The June campaign reused spring creative assets instead of commissioning a new shoot. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-07-09)
Production costs of the pulled-forward October activation; see the matching revenue variance. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-11-08)
Print and media rates increased at the main supplier from March; a rate-card renegotiation is underway. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-04-10)
A brand activation sold for early 2025 was pulled into October at the client's request. The matching cost variance appears on the COGS line the same month. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-11-08)
Contractor spend deferred during the summer staffing gap on the delivery bench; caught up from September. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-08-09)

External development spend paused over the year-end delivery freeze and resumed in February. This recurs every holiday season; budget phasing to be corrected in the next cycle. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-02-10)

External development spend paused over the year-end delivery freeze and resumed in February. This recurs every holiday season; budget phasing to be corrected in the next cycle. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-02-10)
June delivery mix skewed toward license resale rather than custom build, which carries lower external cost. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-07-10)

Two platform go-lives slipped from January into February on client-side content delays. Revenue is recognized on delivery, so January carries the gap; both projects were invoiced in February. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-02-10)
One retainer client commissioned an out-of-cycle campaign microsite in an otherwise quiet August. (FP&A Analyst, 2025-09-09)
Two institutional clients pushed their October milestone invoicing into November after a budget-cycle approval delay on their side. Timing only. (FP&A Analyst, 2024-11-08)
